prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One idea that I believe is overrated in modern knowledge work is the notion of working longer hours under the assumption that more hours worked automatically translates into higher productivity and output quality. The reality is that after a certain point, additional hours worked do not necessarily result in better outcomes, and can even negatively affect an individual's ability to perform at their best.

Humans are not machines that can operate at peak performance for an indefinite period of time. Physical and especially mental fatigue sets in after working for an extended time period, which can lead to a decrease in both the quantity and quality of work performed. This is particularly problematic for knowledge work that relies on creativity, problem-solving, and critical thinking—functions that are highly dependent on an individual's level of alertness and cognitive freshness.

Furthermore, in many fields, the workday is not only the time when actual work (e.g., analysis, writing, programming, or experiment) gets done but also when professionals perform activities essential for keeping up with new developments, exchange knowledge with other individuals in their field, and think about new approaches, methodologies, or technologies they could apply in their work. If professionals are working longer hours, the time and energy they have available for these activities will be significantly reduced or take place at the time when they are already exhausted from their main duties.

Therefore, while working longer hours may be seen as demonstrating great commitment to one's work, it can also be an indicator of poor time management, a lack of focus, or even the inability to deal effectively with the complexity and difficulty of the task at hand. Instead, practitioners in knowledge work should strive for improving their efficiency and effectiveness through techniques such as deep work (the ability to focus completely on your task with no distractions for a significant period of time), a better understanding of the problem or task they are dealing with, and processes and tools that enable them to better utilize their creative and cognitive capabilities. In the end, it is not the quantity but the quality of work that truly matters, and sometimes achieving the best possible outcome means working on a task for fewer hours, not more.
Note: This question and answer relates specifically to the business of creating AI tools for optimizing and improving the performance of knowledge work. The same principles do not necessarily apply to other types of work, such as manual labor, where the time spent on task may be a more direct indicator of productivity and output quality.
